title: Semi-transmitter-device-independent quantum key distribution
authors: Qiang Zeng, Abhishek Mishra, Haoyang Wang, Zhiliang Yuan
abstract: Transmitter-device-dependence is a longstanding but often implicit problem in quantum key distribution (QKD), as compared to measurement-device-dependence. One-sided device-independent (1sDI) scenario relaxes the security conditions of DI framework and offers an intuitive solution to transmitter-sided dependence. Here we show that, by constructing a composable transmitter in a 1sDI configuration, the transmitter-device-dependence can be largely eliminated. Our scheme integrates the entanglement source into the transmitter as an individual part, and the detection module is another individual part treated as black box. In a proof-of-principle experiment we obtain a secure key-rate of 1~kbps at an equivalent transmission fiber distance of 20~km, demonstrating the first discrete-variable 1sDI-QKD. By implementing semi-transmitter-device-independent security while maintaining strong loss tolerance, our approach bridges security and practicality for real-world 1sDI-QKD deployments.
summary: This paper introduces a semi-transmitter-device-independent (STDI) protocol for quantum key distribution to mitigate vulnerabilities in the photon source. By treating the transmitter as a composable unit of an untrusted source and a black-box detector, the authors reduce the stringent requirements of full device-independence. They experimentally demonstrate this protocol using polarization entanglement, achieving a 1 kbps key rate over 20 km of fiber. This approach offers a more practical path toward secure, large-scale quantum communication networks.
main_problem: Addressing transmitter-side vulnerabilities in quantum key distribution (QKD) by formalizing and experimentally demonstrating a semi-transmitter-device-independent (STDI) protocol.
main_result: The authors successfully demonstrated the first discrete-variable 1sDI-QKD protocol, achieving a secure key rate of 1 kbps over an equivalent fiber distance of 20 km.
method: The study uses a composable transmitter approach that integrates an untrusted source with a detection module, utilizing the Devetak-Winter bound and the NPA hierarchy for security verification.
model_or_system: A polarization-entanglement-based system using a Sagnac-loop interferometer with a PPLN crystal for SPDC, featuring superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors (SNSPDs) and active wave plates.
key_observables: Secure key rate, photon detection statistics (single clicks, double clicks, no-clicks), and state fidelity via tomography.
important_parameters: Heralding efficiency (65%), dark count probabilities, detection efficiency (25% simulated), and a critical heralding efficiency threshold of 54.8%.
main_assumptions: The detectors are assumed to be indistinguishable from dark counts, and Bob's detectors are assumed to anti-commute.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the proposed STDI configuration compared to standard DI; Figure 2 shows the experimental setup; Figure 5 shows state tomography results; Figure 6 compares experimental probability distributions with theoretical predictions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of transmitter-side dependence, proposes the STDI protocol, details the experimental setup and detection modeling, presents security analysis via numerical optimization, and concludes with experimental validation and performance metrics.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum networks as it provides a practical bridge between high-security device-independent protocols and the high-loss tolerance required for real-world fiber-based quantum communication.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Transmitter-device-independent configuration. The inputs of Alice are determined by Bob. (b) Semi- transmitter-device-independent configuration. Alice’s mea- surement device is treated as black box, and her inputs are individually selected. The entanglement source is integrated into a composable transmitter, sending one of the entangled photons to the measurement in a one-way manner with the other side being independent of Alice’s inputs.

FIG. 2. Experimental setup. The setup of transmitter is contained in two isolated virtual black boxes indicating the internal mechanism are not postulated. The PPLN crystal dimensions are: 2×1×10 mm3 (W×H×L). Abbreviations: a-HWP, active half wave plate; a-QWP, active quarter wave plate; DM, dichroic mirror; PBS, polarization beam splitter; SNSPD, superconducting nanowire single-photon detector.

FIG. 4. Comparison of our detector and state-of-the-art de- tector at the receiver with respect to the relation between se- cure key-rate and transmission distance from the transmitter to the trusted receiver. The equivalent transmission distance is estimated through a standard telecom optical fiber with a loss of 0.2 dB/km. The red pentagram denotes the estimated secure key-rate based on our experimental data point.

FIG. 3. Key-rate bound by the performance of the trans- mitter. Parameters ηA and v are the heralding efficiency of Alice and the visibility of the entanglement state, respec- tively. The simulation adopts ηB = 25%, pA D = 1 × 10−3, and pB D = 2.6 × 10−3. The red pentagram denotes our experimen- tal data point.
FIG. 5. State tomography results. Reconstructed state density matrix from the experimental data. The state density matrix of the maximally entangled state (|01⟩+ |10⟩) / √
FIG. 6. The results of full probability distribution. The ten gray bars denote the redundant correlation probabilities due to the probability conservation and no-signaling constraints.
